payload.title: Narada should implement MCP as typed authority-preserving facade
payload.summary: Narada should implement an MCP server soon as an execution priority. Multi-agent and multi-Site workflows are already encountering shell quoting friction, Windows/WSL crossing friction, rich envelope payloads, and the need for typed discoverable operations. MCP should not become a second authority surface; it should be a thin typed facade over Narada's canonical application services.
evidence: Recent work repeatedly used CLI commands to submit inbox envelopes, but the ergonomics were fragile: shell quoting, enum mistakes, runtime posture checks, and cross-environment command construction. Upcoming canonical inbox/outbox/routing/capability/file-drop workflows will have richer schemas and are natural MCP tools for agents. The CLI remains trustworthy, but agent ergonomics now need a typed protocol surface.
proposal: Add a Narada MCP server that exposes canonical operations such as inbox submit/list/work-next/promote, outbox compose/preview/approve/send/confirm, task/chapter operations, Site registry/routing lookup, capability inspection, and admission dry-run. The MCP tools must call the same application services as CLI commands, return the same envelope ids and mutation evidence, and never write through a side implementation. MCP should expose schemas and dry-run/approval/execution separation explicitly.
payload.recommendation: Start with read-only and low-risk tools: doctor/status, inbox submit-observation with read-back confirmation, inbox work-next, and Site registry lookup. Then add mutation tools only when they emit the same canonical evidence as CLI. Keep CLI as the durable operator/admin surface and MCP as the typed agent-facing facade.

promotion.note: Envelope archived; no target-zone mutation was performed.
